bifocal.target: Novelty boost in Layer 5: score memories inversely to retrieval frequency, gated by QueryIntent::Exploratory

spec_corrections.note: Feature spec contains 2 factual errors and 1 critical omission about the existing codebase

corrections.claim: access_count is now used as a POSITIVE signal (7% boost via log-scaling in Layer 5)
corrections.actual: TRUE but incomplete. access_count is used as a positive signal in TWO independent paths: (1) recall.rs Layer 5 BRIDGE-1 at 0.07 max additive, AND (2) relevance.rs ProactiveRelevanceEngine at 14% of the full LearnedWeights budget. The novelty signal would only affect path (1). Path (2) — the proactive/auto-context path — would continue rewarding access_count positively. This creates a split-brain: the same memory gets novelty-boosted in explicit recall but access-boosted in proactive surfacing.

corrections.claim: Gate: only apply for exploratory queries ('what do I know about', 'what else', 'anything about')
corrections.actual: MISALIGNED with existing classifier. QueryIntent::Exploratory triggers on 'tell me about', 'explain', 'describe', 'summarize', 'how does', 'why does', 'history of', 'background', 'everything about', 'more about', 'related'. It does NOT trigger on 'what do I know about' (starts with 'what' which is a needle_starter prefix match), 'what else' (same), or 'anything about' (not in any pattern list). The proposed gating keywords and the actual classifier keywords are DISJOINT SETS.

Mathematical note: log(access_count+1) here is ambiguous — ln or log2? The existing access_boost uses ln_1p (natural log), while relevance.rs uses log2. If using ln: novelty=0 at access_count=e^5-1=147.4, so memories need 148+ accesses to get zero novelty. If using log2: novelty=0 at access_count=31. The spec says 'memories with 16+ accesses get 0' which matches NEITHER formula. 16 accesses: ln(17)/5=0.567, novelty=0.013 (not 0). log2(17)/5=0.817, novelty=0.005 (close to 0 but not 0). The spec's stated behavior doesn't match its own formula.

novelty_scoring_in_layer5.reason: Additive signal in existing scoring closure. No new state, no new types, no persistence changes. Reads existing access_count field. Pure arithmetic. Medium risk from score perturbation on critical retrieval path.

query_intent_gating.reason: CRITICAL. The gate determines when novelty fires vs. doesn't. Misclassification means either (a) novelty fires on needle queries, demoting proven-helpful memories for factual lookups, or (b) novelty never fires because the user's actual exploratory patterns don't match the classifier. The existing QueryIntent classifier has KNOWN coverage gaps — it uses starts_with for needle patterns, which means 'What do we know about X' hits 'what' → Needle even though it's exploratory. Same for 'What patterns do you see' → Needle. This is the HIGHEST risk component.

access_count_signal_coherence.reason: CRITICAL. Adding an anti-correlated signal on the same dimension. The net effect of access_count in Layer 5 would become: access_boost(0.07) - novelty_penalty(0.03) = 0.04 net positive for high-access memories on exploratory queries. But for zero-access memories, the effect is: 0(access) + 0.03(novelty) = 0.03 boost. This means the feature's ACTUAL effect is a +0.03 blanket boost to never-accessed memories during exploratory queries, not really 'novelty scoring'. The framing is misleading.

benchmark_regression.reason: LoCoMo benchmark uses fresh memories (access_count=0 for all). Novelty boost would apply +0.03 uniformly to ALL memories during exploratory queries, having ZERO discriminative power. No regression, but also no benefit — the benchmark can't measure this feature at all.

proactive_path_divergence.reason: relevance.rs ProactiveRelevanceEngine would NOT get novelty. Two retrieval paths would rank the same memories differently for the same query intent. User asks 'tell me about X' via MCP recall (gets novelty) vs. auto-context surfaces memories proactively (no novelty). Inconsistent ranking breaks user mental model.

edge_analysis.data_crossing: access_count (u32) read from MemoryMetadata through Arc<Mutex> lock (mem.access_count() call). Same lock path as existing access_boost — no new lock contention.
edge_analysis.control_flow: Pure arithmetic, no branching except the max(0, ...) clamp. Executes inside the per-memory scoring closure (line ~2180), called once per candidate memory. Budget: ~10ns per memory.
edge_analysis.failure_modes: None from arithmetic. ln(0+1)=0, so access_count=0 yields novelty=0.03. Negative results clamped by max(0,...). No division by zero, no overflow.
edge_analysis.contracts: Input: u32 access_count. Output: f32 in [0.0, 0.03]. Contract is tight.

fractal_analysis.pattern_consistency: OTHER signals in Layer 5 use a similar pattern: compute f32, clamp to range, add to final_score. Novelty would match this pattern. CONSISTENT at micro scale.
fractal_analysis.scale_inconsistency: At meso scale, the signal CONTRADICTS its sibling. access_boost and novelty_boost both derive from access_count but pull in opposite directions. No other signal pair in Layer 5 has this property. This is a FRACTAL BREAK — the pattern 'one input, one signal' holds everywhere else. Breaking it introduces a context-dependent sign flip on the same underlying data.
fractal_analysis.mirror_test: If we mirror this to the proactive path (relevance.rs), we'd need a novelty dimension in LearnedWeights that's anti-correlated with access_count. The feedback learning loop (apply_feedback) would then try to learn weights for two anti-correlated dimensions — this is known to cause weight oscillation in linear models. The feature's implicit design decision to NOT mirror to proactive is actually correct, but creates the split-brain problem.

hazop.NO: access_count=0 → novelty=0.03, access_boost=0. Net signal: +0.03. Correct for truly novel memories.
hazop.MORE: access_count=1000 → novelty=0, access_boost=0.07*(ln(1001)/5)=0.097 (clamped to 0.07 by the *0.07 factor). Wait — actually there's no explicit clamp. ln(1001)/5=1.38, *0.07=0.097. This EXCEEDS the stated 0.07 max. The existing access_boost has NO upper clamp beyond the formula. At 1000 accesses, access_boost=0.097. This is a pre-existing issue, not introduced by novelty, but it means the signal budget math is already wrong.
hazop.REVERSE: What if novelty is applied to needle queries by accident (classifier misfire)? A proven-helpful memory (access_count=50) gets novelty=max(0, 1-ln(51)/5)*0.03=max(0,1-0.786)*0.03=0.006. Marginal. A never-accessed memory gets +0.03. On a needle query where the user wants THE answer, promoting an untested memory by 0.03 over the proven one could swap rank positions if scores are within 0.024 of each other.
hazop.PART_OF: Novelty only covers recall path, not proactive. A memory novel in recall context is also novel in proactive context, but only gets the boost in one. Partial application.
hazop.EARLY: First query in a new session — all memories have access_count from prior sessions. Novelty correctly reflects cumulative history. No early/late timing issue.
hazop.LATE: After many recall queries in a session, access_count increments (via update_access_count_instrumented at line 112). Memories retrieved early in the session lose novelty for later queries. This is CORRECT behavior — the signal naturally decays as memories are surfaced.

edge_analysis.data_crossing: query_analysis.intent (QueryIntent enum) computed at line 269 via analyze_query(). Captured by the Layer 5 closure. No additional computation needed.
edge_analysis.control_flow: Single match on QueryIntent::Exploratory. Gate is binary: novelty ON or OFF. No partial application.
edge_analysis.failure_modes: CRITICAL: Misclassification. The QueryIntent classifier uses string prefix matching. Queries starting with 'what' hit needle_starters before exploratory_starters are checked. This means: 'What do we know about security?' → Needle (not Exploratory). 'What patterns do you see?' → Needle. 'What should we focus on?' → Needle (starts with 'what'). Of the 20 LoCoMo queries, 14 start with 'What/Who/When/How' — ALL would classify as Needle or match needle_starters. Only 1 query ('Summarize the project so far') would definitively classify as Exploratory.
edge_analysis.attack_surface: None — query classification is deterministic from query text.

fractal_analysis.pattern_consistency: Other gated signals in Layer 5: session_boost gates on age_hours<=2, temporal_boost gates on has_temporal_query, episode_boost gates on matching episode_id. All use CONTENT-DERIVED gates (properties of the memory or query-memory relationship). Novelty's gate is INTENT-DERIVED (property of the query alone). This is a different gate TYPE. FRACTAL MISMATCH at meso scale — novelty is the only signal gated by query intent classification rather than content/temporal properties.
fractal_analysis.scale_consistency: At macro scale, QueryIntent is used for retrieval STRATEGY selection (BM25/Vector vs Graph weights). Using it for signal gating mixes levels of abstraction — strategy selection is a routing decision, signal gating is a scoring decision. These should be independent.

hazop.NO: QueryIntent::Hybrid (the default) — novelty does NOT fire. This is the most common classification for balanced queries. The feature would NEVER activate for the majority of real-world queries.
hazop.OTHER_THAN: What if we gate on QueryType::Exploratory instead of QueryIntent::Exploratory? QueryType::Exploratory is the default fallback (line 703: 'Default to exploratory'). This means it fires for EVERYTHING that isn't Attribute or Temporal. Too broad — novelty would fire on needle queries. Neither classifier provides the right granularity.

fmea.failure_mode: QueryIntent classifies exploratory query as Needle (false negative)
fmea.note: High probability because the classifier's starts_with('what') catches most English questions. Low detectability because the feature simply doesn't fire — no visible error, just silent no-op. The user doesn't know novelty was supposed to help. RPN 168 = significant risk that the feature is dead code for most queries.

edge_analysis.data_crossing: Both signals read access_count. No data dependency between them — computed independently then summed.
edge_analysis.insight: The net effect is ALWAYS POSITIVE. Novelty never actually penalizes high-access memories — it just gives low-access memories a smaller additional boost during exploratory queries. This is NOT 'surprise scoring'. It's a 'low-access bonus for exploratory queries'. The framing as novelty/surprise is misleading. The actual effect is: on exploratory queries, ALL memories get a slight boost that's larger for less-accessed ones. The maximum differential between a 0-access and 50-access memory is 0.030 — roughly 3% of a typical final_score of ~0.85.

fractal_analysis.pattern: Every other signal in Layer 5 is MONOTONIC — more of the underlying property always means more (or less) score. access_boost is monotonically increasing with access_count. The COMBINED effect of access_boost+novelty is STILL monotonically increasing (see net effect table — net always increases with access_count). The novelty signal merely reduces the SLOPE of the access-count benefit curve for exploratory queries. It doesn't create a non-monotonic effect.
fractal_analysis.implication: The feature does NOT achieve its stated goal of making 'never-retrieved memories rank higher than frequently-retrieved ones'. A memory with access_count=50 still scores higher than one with access_count=0, even on exploratory queries (0.055 > 0.030 — wait, those are access_boost vs novelty individually). Let me recompute the NET: access_count=0 → net=0.030; access_count=50 → net=0.055. The high-access memory STILL wins by 0.025. Novelty closes the gap from 0.055 to 0.025 but does not reverse the ranking.

edge_analysis.divergence: recall.rs would apply novelty; relevance.rs would not. For a query that routes through both paths (e.g., auto-context also uses some recall), the same memory gets different scores.
edge_analysis.practical_impact: Low in the current architecture. Proactive (relevance.rs surface_relevant) and explicit recall (recall.rs recall_with_feedback) are invoked by different code paths — proactive by hooks, recall by MCP tools. They don't compete for the same result set. But if a user sees auto-surfaced context and then asks 'recall' for the same topic, the rankings WILL differ. This is confusing but not harmful.
edge_analysis.future_risk: If paths are unified (e.g., recall falls back to proactive or vice versa), the divergence becomes a bug.

fractal_analysis.pattern: BRIDGE signals (lines 2273-2286) were specifically designed to port proactive signals INTO the recall path to reduce divergence. Novelty goes the OPPOSITE direction — it adds a signal to recall that does NOT exist in proactive. This REVERSES the convergence trajectory of the BRIDGE pattern. FRACTAL BREAK at architectural scale.

phase_2_fractal_and_atam.core_pattern: Layer 5 scoring is an additive ensemble of ~21 orthogonal signals, each contributing [0.0, max_boost] to a base score. Signals are independent, monotonic, and unconditionally applied (or gated by content/temporal properties). Feedback learning happens at the proactive layer.

pattern_breaks.description: novelty_boost is anti-correlated with access_boost on the same input (access_count). No other signal pair in Layer 5 shares an input dimension. This violates the implicit assumption that signals are orthogonal. While the net effect is still monotonic, it means the 'effective access weight' becomes query-intent-dependent, which is a form of hidden interaction that makes the scoring model harder to reason about.

All existing gates are content-derived (temporal match, episode match, recency window). Novelty introduces intent-derived gating. This mixes levels of abstraction. Intent classification errors are invisible (no fallback, no telemetry), making this harder to debug than content-derived gates that can be verified against ground truth.
Signal exists in recall but not proactive. Reverses the BRIDGE convergence pattern. Creates a path where the two scoring systems diverge rather than converge.

trade_offs.name: Novelty vs. Reliability
trade_offs.description: Boosting never-accessed memories surfaces potentially stale, low-quality, or irrelevant content. access_count serves as a PROXY for quality — frequently retrieved memories have been validated by repeated use. Novelty undermines this proxy.
trade_offs.sensitivity_point: When access_count is the ONLY distinguishing signal between two semantically similar memories, novelty flips the tiebreaker on exploratory queries. If the high-access memory was high-access because it's genuinely better, novelty causes a quality regression.

trade_offs.name: Coverage vs. Precision
trade_offs.description: Novelty increases coverage (surfaces memories that would otherwise be buried). This is the stated goal. But in a top-k system (k=5 or k=10), every novel memory that enters the result set DISPLACES a proven memory. For k=5, adding one novel memory is a 20% precision risk.
trade_offs.sensitivity_point: open_domain queries where MRR and R@5 are currently 0.900. If novelty promotes a never-accessed memory into the top-5, it could displace a correct expected answer.

trade_offs.name: Signal Complexity vs. Marginal Value
trade_offs.description: Layer 5 already has 21 signals. The marginal information content of signal #22 is low (diminishing returns). The 0.03 max boost is smaller than session_boost (0.03), activation_boost (0.03 max), and much smaller than access_boost (0.07). The signal's discriminative power between memories is at most 0.030 — within noise range for most queries.

challenge_1_dead_code_risk.claim: Novelty will surface forgotten memories on exploratory queries
challenge_1_dead_code_risk.adversary: The QueryIntent classifier classifies most natural-language questions as Needle (starts_with 'what', 'who', 'when', 'how much'). The exploratory_starters list catches 'tell me about', 'explain', 'describe', 'summarize' — these are RARE in practice. Real users ask questions ('What do I know about...', 'What did we discuss about...'), not commands ('Tell me about...'). Analysis of the 20 LoCoMo queries: only 1 ('Summarize the project so far') definitively classifies as Exploratory. 5 more ('How did...', 'Why did...') might catch via 'how do'/'why do' starters. The remaining 14 are Needle. In production, the fraction of Exploratory queries may be even lower.
challenge_1_dead_code_risk.verdict: Feature fires for <30% of queries in the benchmark, possibly <15% in production. The 0.03 boost on <15% of queries affecting only zero-access memories is a vanishingly small effect size.
challenge_1_dead_code_risk.impact_on_score: Utility dimension reduced from initial estimate

challenge_2_benchmark_blindness.claim: LoCoMo benchmark can validate this feature
challenge_2_benchmark_blindness.adversary: ALL 60 memories in the LoCoMo benchmark are created fresh with access_count=0. Novelty gives ALL of them +0.03 on exploratory queries — a UNIFORM boost that does NOT change relative rankings. The benchmark literally cannot measure this feature's effect. R@5, MRR, P@5 will be IDENTICAL before and after adding novelty.
challenge_2_benchmark_blindness.verdict: No benchmark coverage. Feature is untestable with existing infrastructure. New benchmark needed with variable access_count distribution.

challenge_3_0_03_is_noise.claim: 0.03 max novelty boost can meaningfully change rankings
challenge_3_0_03_is_noise.adversary: Typical final_score for a top-5 memory is 0.75-0.95. Score GAP between rank 5 and rank 6 is typically 0.01-0.05. Novelty's max 0.03 CAN bridge this gap in ~30% of cases (when gap < 0.03). But novelty only fires on exploratory queries (~15-30% of queries) AND only makes a difference for 0-access memories competing against >0-access memories. Combined probability: ~5-10% of all queries might see ANY ranking change. Of those, the change promotes an untested memory — which may or may not be better.
challenge_3_0_03_is_noise.verdict: Expected impact: ranking change in ~5-10% of queries, quality improvement in ~50% of those (net 2.5-5% of queries improved, 2.5-5% degraded). This is within noise for the current benchmark.

challenge_4_interaction_with_feedback_le.adversary: In the proactive path (relevance.rs), feedback learning adjusts access_count weight via gradient descent. If a never-accessed memory is promoted by novelty in recall, retrieved, and then gets POSITIVE feedback, it increments access_count. Next time, it gets less novelty but more access_boost. The feedback loop is STABLE — novelty acts as a bootstrapping mechanism that feeds into access_count over time. This is actually a GOOD interaction. However, if the promoted memory gets NEGATIVE feedback, access_count stays low (it was retrieved once), and novelty STILL promotes it next time. There's no negative feedback path for novelty — bad novelty promotions don't decay.
challenge_4_interaction_with_feedback_le.verdict: One-directional feedback: positive outcomes naturally resolve, negative outcomes persist. This is an asymmetric risk.

correctness.evidence_code: Formula is arithmetically correct. access_count read path is safe (existing Arc<Mutex> pattern). Clamp via max(0,...) prevents negative values. No edge cases in pure arithmetic.
correctness.evidence_structural: BUT: the formula's stated behavior ('memories with 16+ accesses get 0') doesn't match the actual formula output (16 accesses → novelty=0.005, not 0). The spec is internally inconsistent. Additionally, the gating classifier (QueryIntent) will misclassify the majority of exploratory queries as Needle due to starts_with('what') prefix matching.

robustness.evidence_code: No crash paths, no panics, no overflow. Pure additive signal with bounded output [0.0, 0.03]. Gating is binary (no partial states). Memory lock already held for access_count read.
robustness.evidence_structural: Asymmetric feedback risk: bad novelty promotions have no decay mechanism. The feature can persistently promote low-quality memories that happen to have low access_count. No circuit breaker, no telemetry to detect this. The BRIDGE pattern convergence is reversed.

security.evidence_code: No new attack surface. access_count is u32, not user-controlled. QueryIntent is deterministic from query text. No external calls, no I/O.
security.evidence_structural: Memory scoring is internal to the system. No PII, no auth, no external data. Signal is bounded and additive — worst case is minor ranking perturbation.

performance.evidence_code: One ln() call + arithmetic per memory in the scoring closure. Cost: <5ns per memory. With typical 20-candidate rerank pool, total cost <100ns per query. Negligible vs. the ~50ms total query latency.
performance.evidence_structural: No new allocations, no new locks, no new I/O. Pattern matches existing signal computation. No performance risk.

maintainability.evidence_code: Signal #22 in a 21-signal ensemble. Each new signal makes the scoring function harder to reason about. The anti-correlation with access_boost requires understanding BOTH signals to predict net behavior.
maintainability.evidence_structural: The intent-gated novelty creates a conditional scoring regime where some queries use 22 signals and others use 21. No other signal has this property. Debugging score discrepancies now requires checking QueryIntent classification as well as all signal values. The BRIDGE pattern documentation (comments at line 2273) would need updating to explain why novelty goes opposite to the bridge convergence.

data_integrity.evidence_code: Read-only signal. Does not modify access_count or any stored data. Pure function of existing metadata.
data_integrity.evidence_structural: No persistence changes. No schema changes. No migration needed.

scalability.evidence_code: O(1) per memory computation. Scales linearly with candidate pool size (same as all other signals).
scalability.evidence_structural: No new state. No new indexes. No new caches. No scaling concerns.

testability.evidence_code: Unit testing the formula is trivial. Integration testing is not — the LoCoMo benchmark uses access_count=0 for all memories, making novelty a uniform additive with zero discriminative power.
testability.evidence_structural: Testing this feature requires a benchmark with VARIABLE access_count distribution across the corpus. No such benchmark exists. Creating one requires either (a) instrumenting a long-running instance to collect real access patterns, or (b) synthetic access_count assignment, which may not reflect real distribution. The feature is UNTESTABLE with current infrastructure.

completeness.evidence_code: The feature implements a novelty boost in recall.rs but NOT in relevance.rs (proactive path). This is an incomplete integration that creates path divergence.
completeness.evidence_structural: Missing: (1) Telemetry for novelty signal attribution (existing pattern in signal_attributions map, line 2515). (2) Novelty in proactive path or documented rationale for exclusion. (3) Feedback mechanism to learn from bad novelty promotions. (4) Correct benchmark with variable access_count. (5) QueryIntent classifier fixes for 'what do I know about' pattern.

coherence.evidence_code: The feature adds a signal anti-correlated with an existing signal on the same dimension. The net effect (always positive, monotonically increasing) means novelty doesn't actually create novel ranking behavior — it just SOFTENS the access_count advantage on exploratory queries.
coherence.evidence_structural: The stated goal ('surface surprising memories') is not achieved by the proposed implementation. What IS achieved: 'slightly reduce the advantage of frequently-accessed memories on exploratory queries'. These are different features. The naming, framing, and motivation don't match the mathematical reality.

utility.evidence_code: 0.03 max boost on ~15-30% of queries affecting only zero-access memories competing against >0-access memories. Expected ranking impact: ~5-10% of queries see ANY change.
utility.evidence_structural: The feature addresses a real user need (avoid surfacing the same memories repeatedly) but the implementation is too weak and too narrowly gated to deliver measurable improvement. The 0.03 budget is dwarfed by the 0.07 access_boost it's trying to counteract. And the QueryIntent gate excludes most of the queries where users would actually want novelty.

ease_of_use.evidence_code: Zero user-facing changes. No new API parameters, no configuration needed. Automatic behavior change.
ease_of_use.evidence_structural: Users don't need to do anything differently. But they also can't control or observe the behavior. No 'prefer_novel=true' parameter, no way to see novelty attribution in results.

quality.rationale: Clean arithmetic, correct Rust patterns, but spec-implementation mismatch (stated 'zero at 16' doesn't match formula) and anti-correlation with sibling signal is an architectural smell.

completeness.rationale: Recall-only implementation. No proactive path. No telemetry. No benchmark. No feedback loop. Missing QueryIntent fixes for the proposed gate keywords.

performance.rationale: Negligible computational cost. No new allocations, locks, or I/O.

utility.rationale: Expected measurable impact on <5% of queries. Maximum ranking delta (0.03) is within noise for most result sets. Feature does not achieve its stated goal of reversing access-count ranking.

coherence.rationale: Goal-implementation mismatch. Anti-correlated signal on same dimension. Intent-gating mixes abstraction levels. Reverses BRIDGE convergence pattern.

cohesiveness.rationale: Fits the additive-signal pattern of Layer 5 at micro scale. Breaks fractal consistency at meso (anti-correlated sibling) and macro (path divergence) scales.

elasticity.rationale: The 0.03 budget can be tuned. The gate could be changed to a different classifier. The formula could be adjusted. But the fundamental tension (anti-correlation with access_boost) cannot be tuned away — it's structural.

function.rationale: Does not achieve stated function. Stated: 'surface surprising/novel memories'. Actual: 'give a tiny bonus to zero-access memories on exploratory queries'. These are different functions.

ease_of_use.rationale: Automatic, no user configuration needed. But also no user control or observability.

q1_does_novelty_conflict_with_access_cou.answer: YES, structurally but not practically. They are anti-correlated on the same input dimension, which is a fractal break in the signal architecture. However, the net effect is still monotonically increasing with access_count (high-access memories still score higher). Novelty doesn't reverse rankings — it just narrows the gap by 0.030 max. The conflict is architectural (bad precedent, harder to reason about) rather than functional (no actual harm).

q2_coherent_way_to_have_both.answer: YES, but it requires reframing. Instead of adding novelty as a SEPARATE signal, modify the access_boost formula to be QUERY-INTENT-DEPENDENT: on exploratory queries, use a flatter curve (e.g., *0.03 instead of *0.07). This achieves the same effect (less advantage for high-access memories on exploratory queries) without introducing an anti-correlated signal. One signal, two modes. This is coherent and matches the existing pattern where recency_scale varies by query intent (lines 2175-2178).

q3_is_query_intent_gating_sufficient.answer: NO. QueryIntent::Exploratory fires for <30% of LoCoMo queries and likely <15% of production queries. The proposed gate keywords ('what do I know about', 'what else', 'anything about') are NOT in the classifier's pattern lists. The gate would need to be either (a) a new classifier that specifically detects novelty-appropriate queries, or (b) an expansion of the existing Exploratory patterns to include 'what do' and 'what patterns' and 'what should' style questions. Neither is proposed.

q4_locomo_benchmark_impact.answer: ZERO IMPACT. All 60 LoCoMo memories have access_count=0. Novelty applies +0.03 uniformly to all of them on exploratory queries. Uniform additive boost does not change relative rankings. MRR, R@5, R@10, P@5 will be IDENTICAL before and after. The benchmark cannot validate or invalidate this feature.

q5_does_0_03_budget_make_sense.answer: NO. Against the 0.07 access_boost, a 0.03 novelty can never reverse the ranking of a high-access vs. zero-access memory. The maximum differential created by novelty alone (0.030) is less than the access_boost of a memory accessed just 3 times (ln(4)/5*0.07=0.019... wait, let me recompute: ln(4)=1.386, /5=0.277, *0.07=0.019). Actually, 0.030 > 0.019, so novelty CAN outweigh access_boost for memories with 1-3 accesses. But for 5+ accesses (ln(6)/5*0.07=0.025), the gap narrows. At 8 accesses (ln(9)/5*0.07=0.031), access_boost exceeds novelty's maximum. So the crossover point is ~7-8 accesses. The 0.03 budget only 'works' against memories accessed fewer than ~8 times. For the stated scenario (0 vs. 50 accesses), it's completely insufficient.

q6_where_would_this_break.answer: Three edge cases create harm: (1) INFORMATION RETRIEVAL LOOP: A low-quality memory with access_count=0 gets novelty-boosted, retrieved, increments to access_count=1, gets slightly less novelty next time but is still boosted. If user gives no feedback, it slowly accumulates accesses while being unhelpful. No negative feedback path. (2) CLASSIFIER MISFIRE ON FACTUAL QUERIES: 'What do I know about the API authentication flow?' is Exploratory in intent but classified as Needle (starts with 'what'). If the classifier is fixed to catch this, a zero-access memory about API auth could outrank the proven-helpful one the user expects. (3) COLD START AFTER BULK IMPORT: If a user imports 500 memories from another system, all have access_count=0. On exploratory queries, ALL 500 get +0.03, providing zero discrimination. When some get accessed and others don't, the accessed ones lose novelty AND gain access_boost — double penalty for being useful. This inverts quality signal for the import cohort.

recommendation.rationale: The feature has three compounding problems: (1) the mathematical effect doesn't achieve the stated goal (ranking reversal impossible), (2) the gate fires too rarely to matter, and (3) no benchmark can measure it. The idea is sound — novelty IS a valuable retrieval signal. But the implementation needs fundamental redesign.

alternative_proposals.name: A: Query-intent-dependent access scaling (RECOMMENDED)
alternative_proposals.description: Instead of adding a new signal, modify the EXISTING access_boost to use a lower coefficient on exploratory queries. Change line 2278 from `* 0.07` to `* if is_exploratory { 0.02 } else { 0.07 }`. This achieves the same effect (less access advantage on exploratory queries) without anti-correlated signals, without a new signal in the ensemble, and with zero risk of ranking reversal on needle queries.
alternative_proposals.risk: Minimal. Reduces an existing signal's magnitude conditionally.

alternative_proposals.name: B: Diversity-aware MMR with access-count penalty
alternative_proposals.description: Use access_count as a DIVERSITY signal in the existing Layer 5.4 MMR step (lines 2636-2667). When selecting diverse results, penalize candidates that are both semantically similar to already-selected results AND have high access_count. This uses the existing MMR infrastructure and achieves genuine novelty: it pushes low-access memories into the result set when they provide non-redundant information.
alternative_proposals.effort: 1-2 hours. Modify MMR lambda calculation to factor in access_count.
alternative_proposals.risk: Medium. MMR is well-understood but the interaction with access_count needs empirical validation.

alternative_proposals.name: C: Full novelty signal with corrected design
alternative_proposals.description: If novelty as a separate signal is desired: (1) REMOVE access_boost, replace with a single access-novelty signal that's positive for low-count and negative for high-count (net-zero sum). (2) Gate on a NEW 'discovery_mode' flag in the Query struct, not on QueryIntent classification. (3) Add to BOTH recall and proactive paths. (4) Create a benchmark with synthetic access_count distribution. (5) Add signal attribution telemetry.
alternative_proposals.effort: 1-2 days. Significant design and testing work.
alternative_proposals.risk: Medium-high. New signal, new gating, new benchmarks needed.

minimum_requirements: Fix the formula to match stated behavior (use log2, not ln, to get zero at ~31 accesses)
Add 'what do' and 'what patterns' to exploratory_starters in query_parser.rs
Add signal attribution: entry.novelty_contribution = novelty in the signal_attributions map
Add telemetry: NOVELTY_SIGNAL_ACTIVE counter to track how often the gate fires
Create a benchmark variant with access_count distribution: 40% at 0, 20% at 1-5, 20% at 6-20, 20% at 20+
Document the architectural decision to NOT mirror to proactive path
